The sea had always been a whisperer, murmuring secrets to those who dared to listen. But in the small coastal town of Marrow's End, it had turned into a predator.

Detective Elias Vane arrived at dawn, the salt air sharp in his lungs and the weight of the case heavy in his bones. The town was a skeletal thing, perched on the edge of the world, its buildings hunched against the wind like old men. The last few weeks had seen three disappearances—each more unsettling than the last. A fisherman, a schoolteacher, and a young woman who had vanished from her own porch, as if the sea had reached out and pulled her under without a sound.

Vane had seen his share of mysteries, but this felt different. The townspeople spoke in hushed tones, eyes darting when he approached. They avoided his gaze, as if guilt or fear had settled in their bones. The only clue was a single boot print in the sand near the cliffs, half-buried by the tide, its sole worn and frayed.

He walked the shoreline, the waves lapping at his ankles, each step a question. The sea had always been a presence here, but now it felt watchful, as though it knew something he didn’t. The wind carried the scent of brine and something else—something metallic, like blood.

Detective Mara Voss had heard the stories before—of the missing, the vanished, the ones who never came back. But this time, something felt different.

The official report said he’d drowned, but Mara knew better. Drowning didn’t leave you with your eyes wide open like that.
